era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Mesopotamia. The Old Kingdom of Egypt (c. 2686–2181 BCE) is in its later phases; the Fourth Dynasty has ended and the Fifth Dynasty is established. In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic Period III is underway, with competing city-states in Sumer and Akkad. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase. Writing systems (cuneiform, hieroglyphics) are well established and expanding in administrative use.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies, sparse archaeological coverage)
East Asia beyond the Indus Valley (limited contemporary records)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Khafre, Fourth Dynasty Egypt (traditional dating)
events.description: Khafre (also Chephren) is traditionally dated to c. 2558–2532 BCE by conventional Egyptology, but some chronologies place him later. He is credited with constructing the Great Sphinx of Giza and the second pyramid of the Giza complex. His reign represents the apex of Old Kingdom pyramid construction and centralized royal authority. The dating of this reign remains contested among Egyptologists, with some proposing dates as late as 2500 BCE.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza pyramid complex

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom is debated. Conventional dating places Khafre c. 2558–2532 BCE, but absolute dating via radiocarbon and astronomical methods suggests possible shifts of ±50–100 years. Assignment to -2830 is approximate and reflects uncertainty in absolute chronology.

events.title: Mature Indus Valley Civilization urban centers
events.region: Indus Valley, South Asia (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization is at its peak, with major urban centers such as Harappa and Mohenjo-daro fully developed. These cities exhibit sophisticated urban planning, standardized weights and measures, and an undeciphered writing system. Trade networks extend to Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf. The civilization shows no evidence of monumental temples or palaces, suggesting a different social organization than contemporary Egypt or Mesopotamia.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and other sites

events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's mature phase is well-attested archaeologically. Absolute dating via radiocarbon places the mature phase c. 2600–1900 BCE, with -2830 falling within this range.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Khafre to c. 2558–2532 BCE is increasingly questioned by scholars using radiocarbon and astronomical methods, who suggest the Fourth Dynasty may be 50–150 years later than traditional Egyptological chronology. This would place -2830 outside Khafre's reign entirely. No disconfirming evidence identified for the Indus Valley Civilization's mature phase.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2830 is heavily dependent on Egyptian king lists and archaeological stratigraphy, both of which have limitations. Egyptian chronology remains one of the most contested areas in ancient history, with competing 'high,' 'middle,' and 'low' chronologies differing by over a century. The Indus Valley Civilization is known only through archaeology; no contemporary written records have been deciphered. Mesopotamian records for this period are fragmentary. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from the written record.

graph_edges.note: Khafre's reign follows Khufu's; both part of Fourth Dynasty pyramid-building tradition
Indus Valley Civilization mature phase spans multiple centuries
